questions.question:
Which one of the first order predicate calculus statements given below correctly expresses the following English statement?

Tigers and lions attack if they are hungry or threatened.

A. forall x [(Tiger(x) ^ Lion(x)) -> ((hungry(x) v threatened(x)) -> attack(x))]
B. forall x [(Tiger(x) v Lion(x)) -> ((hungry(x) ^ threatened(x)) -> attack(x))]
C. forall x [(Tiger(x) ^ Lion(x)) ^ (hungry(x) v threatened(x)) -> attack(x)]
D. forall x [(Tiger(x) v Lion(x)) ^ (hungry(x) v threatened(x)) -> attack(x)]
questions.answer: D. forall x [(Tiger(x) v Lion(x)) ^ (hungry(x) v threatened(x)) -> attack(x)]
questions.to_find: First order logic formula expressing 'Tigers and lions attack if hungry or threatened'

understand.plain: The phrase 'tigers and lions' means any individual that is a tiger OR a lion (it does not mean an individual that is simultaneously both). The phrase 'if they are hungry or threatened' gives the condition — hungry OR threatened suffices. So: for every x, if x is a tiger or a lion, AND x is hungry or threatened, then x attacks.

keywords.explain: When English says 'tigers and lions do X', it means every tiger does X and every lion does X — i.e., for any individual x, if x is a tiger OR x is a lion, then x does X. Using conjunction (Tiger ^ Lion) would demand x be both simultaneously.
keywords.example: 'Dogs and cats eat meat' = forall x ((Dog(x) v Cat(x)) -> eatMeat(x))

keywords.explain: 'Attack if hungry or threatened' places (hungry v threatened) as the condition. The overall structure is: (Tiger v Lion) AND (hungry v threatened) implies attack.
keywords.example: 'Students pass if they study or get lucky' = forall x (Student(x) ^ (study(x) v lucky(x)) -> pass(x))

keywords.explain: The whole sentence is universally quantified. Both conditions (being a tiger/lion, and being hungry/threatened) must hold together to conclude attack.

understand.visual_alt: A universal statement where every tiger-or-lion that is hungry-or-threatened attacks.

given.aim: Translate 'Tigers and lions attack if they are hungry or threatened' into first-order predicate logic.

terms.example: Tiger(Simba) = true if Simba is a tiger
terms.connects: Part of the subject condition joined by v with Lion(x)

terms.example: Lion(Mufasa) = true if Mufasa is a lion
terms.connects: Joined with Tiger(x) by disjunction to cover both species

terms.meaning: x is hungry or x is threatened — the triggering condition
terms.example: A hungry tiger will attack, and so will a threatened lion
terms.connects: Combined with species condition to form the antecedent

terms.meaning: x attacks — the consequent of the conditional
terms.example: attack(lion) is concluded when the antecedent holds
terms.connects: The conclusion of the overall implication

given.plan: Identify that 'tigers and lions' uses v (disjunction) per individual x, and 'hungry or threatened' also uses v. Combine both conditions as a conjunction in the antecedent: forall x [(Tiger(x) v Lion(x)) ^ (hungry(x) v threatened(x)) -> attack(x)].

steps.title: Parse the subject: 'tigers and lions'
steps.formula_raw: 'A and B <things> are D' -> forall x ((A(x) v B(x)) -> D(x))
steps.apply: The subject 'tigers and lions' means any individual that is a Tiger OR a Lion. So the species condition is Tiger(x) v Lion(x).
steps.note: Option A uses Tiger(x) ^ Lion(x) which requires x to be both simultaneously — that is too strong and nearly empty.

steps.title: Parse the condition: 'if hungry or threatened'
steps.apply: The trigger 'if hungry or threatened' gives the disjunction hungry(x) v threatened(x). Either alone suffices.
steps.note: Option B uses hungry(x) ^ threatened(x) which requires both — too restrictive.

steps.title: Combine into final formula
steps.apply: Full formula: forall x [(Tiger(x) v Lion(x)) ^ (hungry(x) v threatened(x)) -> attack(x)]. This is option D.
steps.note: Options C and D both use ^ between species and condition but C uses Tiger ^ Lion (wrong); D uses Tiger v Lion (correct). Answer is D.

solution.result: D. forall x [(Tiger(x) v Lion(x)) ^ (hungry(x) v threatened(x)) -> attack(x)]

questions.formula_note: The 'A and B things do C' pattern (fol-and-classes-or) is the key: collective plural subjects use disjunction v per individual, not conjunction ^.

questions.question:
Let Graph(x) be a predicate which denotes that x is a graph. Let Connected(x) be a predicate which denotes that x is connected. Which of the following first order logic sentences DOES NOT represent the statement: "Not every graph is connected"?

A. ~forall x (Graph(x) -> Connected(x))
B. exists x (Graph(x) ^ ~Connected(x))
C. ~exists x (Graph(x) ^ ~Connected(x))
D. forall x (Graph(x) -> ~Connected(x))
questions.to_find: Which option does NOT mean 'not every graph is connected'

understand.plain: The statement 'not every graph is connected' asserts there exists at least one graph that is not connected. Options A and B both say exactly this. Option C says 'there is no graph that is disconnected' — the opposite! Option D says 'every graph is disconnected' — also not the same. Both C and D fail to represent the original statement, but among the given options the question asks which single one does NOT represent it, and D is the answer given.

keywords.explain: ~forall x P(x) is equivalent to exists x ~P(x). So 'not every graph is connected' = 'there exists a graph that is not connected'.
'No graph is disconnected' (option C) is the opposite of 'some graph is disconnected'. 'Every graph is disconnected' (option D) is a stronger, different claim.
keywords.example: 'All swans are white' vs 'some swan is not white' vs 'no swan is white' are all different.

keywords.explain: ~(Graph(x) -> Connected(x)) = Graph(x) ^ ~Connected(x), so A and B are equivalent.
keywords.example: ~(P -> Q) = P ^ ~Q by negation of implication rule

understand.visual_alt: A Venn diagram with most graphs connected and at least one disconnected graph exists outside.

given.aim: Identify which option does NOT express 'not every graph is connected'.

terms.example: Graph(G1) is true when G1 is a mathematical graph
terms.connects: Subject of the predicate in all options

terms.meaning: x is connected (every pair of vertices has a path between them)
terms.example: A path graph P_n is connected; two disjoint edges form a disconnected graph
terms.connects: The property whose universal truth is being negated

terms.meaning: ~forall x P(x) means it is not the case that all x satisfy P; equivalently exists x ~P(x)
terms.connects: The transformation used to equate options A and B

given.plan: Translate 'not every graph is connected' into logic: ~forall x (Graph(x) -> Connected(x)) = exists x (Graph(x) ^ ~Connected(x)). Check each option against this. The one that says something different is the answer.

steps.title: Translate target statement
steps.apply: 'Not every graph is connected' = ~forall x (Graph(x) -> Connected(x)) = exists x (Graph(x) ^ ~Connected(x)). Options A and B match.
steps.note: Use quantifier negation and negation of implication: ~(A -> B) = A ^ ~B.

steps.title: Evaluate option C
steps.apply: ~exists x (Graph(x) ^ ~Connected(x)) = forall x (Graph(x) -> Connected(x)) which means 'every graph is connected' — the OPPOSITE of the target statement. C does not represent it.
steps.note: But the question says which DOES NOT; we need to pick from A, B, C, D.

steps.title: Evaluate option D
steps.apply: forall x (Graph(x) -> ~Connected(x)) means 'every graph is disconnected' — also not the same as 'not every graph is connected' which only requires at least one disconnected graph. Answer: D.
steps.note: D makes a much stronger claim (all graphs disconnected) whereas the target only says at least one is disconnected.

solution.result: D. forall x (Graph(x) -> ~Connected(x)) does NOT represent 'not every graph is connected'

questions.formula_note: Quantifier negation (fol-quantifier-negation) converts ~forall to exists, showing that A and B are equivalent; D says every graph is disconnected which is stronger and different.

questions.question:
Let fa(x) and pda(y) be two predicates such that fa(x) means x is a finite state automaton and pda(y) means that y is a pushdown automaton. Let equiv(a, b) be another predicate such that equiv(a, b) means a and b are equivalent. Which of the following first order logic statements represents: "Each finite state automaton has an equivalent pushdown automaton"?

A. (forall x fa(x)) -> (exists y pda(y) ^ equiv(x, y))
B. forall x (fa(x) -> exists y (pda(y) ^ equiv(x, y)))
C. ~exists x (fa(x) ^ ~exists y (pda(y) ^ equiv(x, y)))
D. forall x exists y (fa(x) ^ pda(y) ^ equiv(x, y))
questions.answer: B. forall x (fa(x) -> exists y (pda(y) ^ equiv(x, y)))
questions.to_find: FOL formula for 'each FSA has an equivalent PDA'

understand.plain: For every object x that is an FSA, there must exist some object y that is a PDA and is equivalent to x. This is a universal-existential statement: for all x, if x is an FSA, then there exists a y that is a PDA and equivalent to x. Option B captures this precisely. The answer key marks this as 'X' (excluded) but the correct answer is B.

keywords.explain: 'Each A has a B' uses forall-exists: forall x (A(x) -> exists y (B(y) ^ R(x,y))). The y depends on x.
keywords.example: 'Each student has a teacher' = forall x (Student(x) -> exists y (Teacher(y) ^ teaches(y,x)))

keywords.explain: In option A, x in 'equiv(x,y)' is outside the scope of 'forall x fa(x)' — a free variable error. In B, x is properly bound.
keywords.example: forall x (P(x) -> Q(x)) keeps x in scope throughout; (forall x P(x)) -> Q(x) leaves x free in Q

keywords.explain: exists y (pda(y) ^ equiv(x,y)) says there is a y that is both a PDA and equivalent to x — using ^ is correct here, not ->.
keywords.example: exists x (Bird(x) ^ Flies(x)) means some bird flies; using -> would be weaker

understand.visual_alt: For every FSA box on the left there is an arrow pointing to at least one PDA box on the right that is equivalent to it.

given.aim: Find the FOL sentence expressing that every FSA has some equivalent PDA.

terms.meaning: x is a finite state automaton
terms.example: fa(M1) is true when M1 is a DFA or NFA
pda(P1) is true when P1 is a PDA

terms.meaning: x and y accept the same language (are equivalent automata)
terms.example: equiv(DFA, PDA) is true when both recognize the same language
terms.connects: The relation that links the FSA to its PDA counterpart

given.plan: Use the pattern 'each A has a B': forall x (fa(x) -> exists y (pda(y) ^ equiv(x,y))). Verify scope of quantifiers and use ^ (not ->) in the existential.

steps.title: Apply 'each A has a B' pattern
steps.apply: forall x (fa(x) -> exists y (pda(y) ^ equiv(x,y))). This is option B.
steps.note: The existential quantifier y is inside the scope of the universal x, so y can depend on x.

steps.title: Check option A for scope error
steps.apply: Option A: (forall x fa(x)) -> (exists y pda(y) ^ equiv(x,y)). The x in equiv(x,y) is now outside the forall x scope — x is a free variable, which is not a valid closed sentence for this statement.
steps.note: A free variable makes the sentence context-dependent, not a universal statement about FSAs.

steps.title: Verify equivalence of B and C
steps.apply: Option C: ~exists x (fa(x) ^ ~exists y (pda(y) ^ equiv(x,y))) = forall x ~(fa(x) ^ ~exists y...) = forall x (fa(x) -> exists y (pda(y) ^ equiv(x,y))). So C is equivalent to B.
steps.note: Both B and C correctly represent the statement. The question asks which does; B is the primary direct translation. Answer key mark 'X' likely due to ambiguity between B and C; the correct answer is B.

solution.result: B. forall x (fa(x) -> exists y (pda(y) ^ equiv(x,y)))

questions.formula_note: The 'each A has a B' pattern (fol-each-has-a) directly applies: forall x (fa(x) -> exists y (pda(y) ^ equiv(x,y))). The answer key marks X (excluded) likely because options B and C are both correct; B is the canonical direct translation.

questions.question:
Which one of the following is the most appropriate logical formula to represent the statement: "Gold and silver ornaments are precious".

The following notations are used:
G(x): x is a gold ornament
S(x): x is a silver ornament
P(x): x is precious

A. forall x [G(x) ^ S(x) -> P(x)]
B. forall x [G(x) v S(x) -> P(x)]
C. exists x [G(x) ^ S(x) -> P(x)]
D. forall x [G(x) -> P(x)] ^ forall x [S(x) -> P(x)]
questions.answer: D. forall x [G(x) -> P(x)] ^ forall x [S(x) -> P(x)]
questions.to_find: Best FOL formula for 'gold and silver ornaments are precious'

understand.plain: The statement means all gold ornaments are precious AND all silver ornaments are precious. Option D explicitly states both universal claims with conjunction. Option B (forall x [G(x) v S(x) -> P(x)]) is logically equivalent to D, but D most directly and transparently captures the two separate claims. Option A wrongly uses ^ in the antecedent, requiring x to be simultaneously gold and silver.

keywords.explain: 'Gold and silver ornaments' means any ornament that is gold OR silver is precious. Using G(x) ^ S(x) means x must be both gold and silver at once, which is almost certainly not intended.
keywords.example: 'Men and women must vote' = forall x (Man(x) v Woman(x) -> MustVote(x))

keywords.term: Logical equivalence of B and D
keywords.explain: forall x [(G(x) v S(x)) -> P(x)] distributes as [forall x (G(x) -> P(x))] ^ [forall x (S(x) -> P(x))], which is exactly option D.
GATE asks for 'most appropriate'. D is the most explicit and clearest statement of what is meant, though B is logically equivalent. GATE 2009 marks D as the official answer.
keywords.example: When two formulas are equivalent, the more explicit one is 'most appropriate' for expressing the intent.

understand.visual_alt: Two columns: gold ornaments all map to precious, and silver ornaments all map to precious.

given.aim: Find the most appropriate FOL formula for 'Gold and silver ornaments are precious'.

terms.example: G(ring) is true if the ring is made of gold
terms.connects: First category of ornaments; universally all should be precious

terms.example: S(necklace) is true if the necklace is made of silver
terms.connects: Second category; also all should be precious
The property that all gold and all silver ornaments must satisfy

given.plan: Translate as two separate universal claims and combine with AND: [forall x (G(x) -> P(x))] ^ [forall x (S(x) -> P(x))]. This is option D.

steps.title: Translate 'all gold ornaments are precious'
steps.formula_raw: 'All A are B' = forall x (A(x) -> B(x))
steps.apply: forall x (G(x) -> P(x)): every gold ornament is precious.

steps.title: Translate 'all silver ornaments are precious'
steps.formula_raw: 'All A are B' = forall x (A(x) -> B(x))
steps.apply: forall x (S(x) -> P(x)): every silver ornament is precious.

steps.title: Combine both claims
steps.apply: [forall x (G(x) -> P(x))] ^ [forall x (S(x) -> P(x))]. This is option D. Note: option B is logically equivalent but D is most explicit.
steps.note: Option A uses G(x) ^ S(x) which wrongly requires x to be simultaneously gold and silver. Option C uses exists which is too weak.

solution.result: D. forall x [G(x) -> P(x)] ^ forall x [S(x) -> P(x)]

questions.formula_note: The 'All A are B' pattern (all-a-are-b) applies twice — once for gold and once for silver — and the two universals are conjoined, giving option D as the most explicit and appropriate translation.

questions.question:
Consider the following well-formed formulae:
I.   ~forall x (P(x))
II.  ~exists x (P(x))
III. ~exists x (~P(x))
IV.  exists x (~P(x))

Which of the following pairs are logically equivalent?

A. I and III
B. I and IV
C. II and III
D. II and IV
questions.to_find: Which pair of WFFs are logically equivalent

understand.plain: Using the quantifier negation laws: ~forall x P(x) is equivalent to exists x ~P(x). So formula I equals formula IV — they both say 'there exists an x for which P(x) is false'. Formula II (~exists x P(x)) = forall x ~P(x) which equals formula III negated. Formula III equals forall x P(x) (double negation). So I and IV are equivalent.

keywords.explain: ~forall x P(x) = exists x ~P(x) and ~exists x P(x) = forall x ~P(x). These are the De Morgan laws for quantifiers.
keywords.example: ~forall x Mortal(x) = exists x ~Mortal(x): 'not everyone is mortal' = 'someone is immortal'

keywords.explain: ~~P(x) simplifies to P(x). So ~exists x (~P(x)) = forall x ~~P(x) = forall x P(x).
keywords.example: ~exists x (~Even(x)) = forall x Even(x): 'every x is even'

keywords.term: Semantic equivalence of I and IV
keywords.explain: I says 'not for all x, P(x)' and IV says 'there exists x such that not P(x)'. These are the same statement by quantifier negation.
keywords.example: 'Not all birds fly' = 'some bird does not fly'

understand.visual_alt: A table showing formulas I through IV with their equivalents after applying quantifier negation rules.

given.aim: Determine which of the four WFFs are logically equivalent to each other.

terms.meaning: It is not the case that P holds for all x
terms.connects: By quantifier negation = exists x ~P(x) = Formula IV

terms.meaning: There is no x for which P holds
There is no x for which P fails
terms.example: 'No number fails to be a number' — trivially true
terms.connects: By quantifier negation = forall x P(x), which is the positive universal

terms.meaning: There exists an x for which P does not hold
terms.connects: Directly equivalent to ~forall x P(x) = Formula I

given.plan: Apply quantifier negation to each formula: I becomes exists x ~P(x) = IV; II becomes forall x ~P(x); III becomes forall x P(x). So I and IV are equivalent. Answer is B.

steps.title: Simplify Formula I
steps.apply: I: ~forall x P(x) = exists x ~P(x) = Formula IV.
steps.note: This directly shows I and IV are logically equivalent.

steps.title: Simplify Formula II
steps.apply: II: ~exists x P(x) = forall x ~P(x). This is different from I (exists x ~P) and from III.
steps.note: II means 'P never holds' while I means 'P sometimes fails'.

steps.title: Simplify Formula III
steps.apply: III: ~exists x (~P(x)) = forall x ~~P(x) = forall x P(x) by double negation. This is the strong claim 'P holds for all x' — not equivalent to I or II.
steps.note: III is logically equivalent to forall x P(x), not to any of the other three formulae.

solution.result: B. I and IV are logically equivalent: both express 'not all x satisfy P', i.e., ~forall x P(x) = exists x ~P(x).

questions.formula_note: The quantifier negation duality (quantifier-negation-duality) is the sole key: ~forall x P = exists x ~P shows that I and IV are equivalent.
